---
title: "Learn R — Interactive Beginner Tutorial"
author: "Your Name"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    self_contained: true
    toc: true
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    toc_depth: 2
    theme: flatly
    highlight: kate
    css: style.css
    includes:
      in_header: header.html
      after_body: footer.html
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)

# Helper function: renders an interactive WebR example block
# Students can READ the code and click Run — field is not editable
example_block <- function(code, id, lang = "r") {
  escaped <- gsub("'", "&#39;", gsub('"', "&quot;", code))
  htmltools::HTML(paste0('
<div class="webr-wrap example-wrap">
  <div class="block-header">
    <span class="block-tag example-tag">&#9654; Example</span>
    <button class="run-btn" onclick="runWebR(\'', id, '\', this)">&#9654; Run</button>
  </div>
  <textarea id="editor-', id, '" class="code-area" readonly>', escaped, '</textarea>
  <div id="out-', id, '" class="output-area" style="display:none;">
    <div class="output-label">## Output</div>
    <div class="output-text"></div>
    <div class="output-plot"></div>
  </div>
</div>
'))
}

# Helper function: renders an editable practice block
# Students WRITE their own code and run it
practice_block <- function(placeholder, id, prompt, hint = NULL) {
  escaped_ph <- gsub("'", "&#39;", gsub('"', "&quot;", placeholder))
  hint_html <- if (!is.null(hint)) {
    paste0('
    <button class="hint-btn" onclick="toggleHint(\'hint-', id, '\')">&#128161; Show Hint</button>
    <div id="hint-', id, '" class="hint-box" style="display:none;">', hint, '</div>')
  } else ""

  htmltools::HTML(paste0('
<div class="webr-wrap practice-wrap">
  <div class="practice-header">
    <span class="practice-tag">&#9999;&#65039; Practice</span>
    <p class="practice-prompt">', prompt, '</p>
  </div>
  <div class="block-header practice-code-header">
    <span class="block-tag practice-code-tag">&#9654; Your Code</span>
    <button class="run-btn practice-run-btn" onclick="runWebR(\'', id, '\', this)">&#9654; Run Code</button>
  </div>
  <textarea id="editor-', id, '" class="code-area practice-area">', escaped_ph, '</textarea>
  <div id="out-', id, '" class="output-area" style="display:none;">
    <div class="output-label">## Output</div>
    <div class="output-text"></div>
    <div class="output-plot"></div>
  </div>', hint_html, '
</div>
'))
}
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
# Display the WebR status banner at the top of the page
htmltools::HTML('
<div id="webr-status-bar">
  <span class="status-dot" id="status-dot"></span>
  <span id="status-msg">&#8987; Loading R in your browser — please wait...</span>
</div>
')
```

---

> **No R installation needed!** This page uses [WebR](https://webr.r-wasm.org/) to run real R code directly in your browser. Click **▶ Run** on any example, and type your own code in the **Practice** blocks.

---

# Chapter 1 — Welcome to R

**R** is a programming language designed for statistics, data analysis, and visualization. It is free, open-source, and used by millions of data scientists, researchers, and analysts worldwide.

Let's write our very first R program — the classic "Hello, World!":

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'print("Hello, World!")
print("Welcome to R programming!")',
  id = "ex1_hello"
)
```

R can also be used as a powerful calculator right out of the box:

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'2 + 2
sqrt(144)
pi
exp(1)    # Euler\'s number e',
  id = "ex1_calc"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
practice_block(
  placeholder = '# Print your name
print("Your Name Here")

# Calculate hours in a year
365 * 24',
  id = "pr1",
  prompt = "Print your own name using <code>print()</code>, then calculate how many hours are in a year (365 × 24).",
  hint = "Use <code>print(\"Your Name\")</code> with your actual name inside the quotes.
          Then on a new line, type <code>365 * 24</code>."
)
```

---

# Chapter 2 — Arithmetic

R supports all standard arithmetic operators:

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `+` | Addition | `5 + 3` | 8 |
| `-` | Subtraction | `10 - 4` | 6 |
| `*` | Multiplication | `3 * 7` | 21 |
| `/` | Division | `15 / 4` | 3.75 |
| `^` | Exponent | `2^8` | 256 |
| `%%` | Modulo (remainder) | `17 %% 5` | 2 |
| `%/%` | Integer division | `17 %/% 5` | 3 |

> 💡 **Tip:** `#` starts a **comment** — R ignores everything after it on that line. Use comments to explain your code!

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'100 + 55       # addition
200 - 87       # subtraction
12 * 9         # multiplication
144 / 12       # division
2 ^ 10         # 2 to the power of 10
17 %% 5        # remainder: 17 divided by 5
17 %/% 5       # integer division: whole part only',
  id = "ex2_arith"
)
```

R follows standard math order of operations (PEMDAS):

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'2 + 3 * 4      # multiplication first → 14
(2 + 3) * 4    # parentheses first → 20
10 / 2 + 3     # division first → 8
10 / (2 + 3)   # parentheses first → 2',
  id = "ex2_order"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
practice_block(
  placeholder = '# Area of a circle with radius 7
# Hint: R has a built-in constant called pi
pi * 7^2

# How many complete weeks in 100 days?
100 %/% 7',
  id = "pr2",
  prompt = "Calculate: (a) the area of a circle with radius 7 (π × r²), and (b) how many complete weeks fit in 100 days.",
  hint = "R has <code>pi</code> built-in. Area = <code>pi * 7^2</code>.
          For whole weeks: <code>100 %/% 7</code> (integer division)."
)
```

---

# Chapter 3 — Variables

A **variable** stores a value so you can use it later. In R, the assignment operator is `<-`.

> 📌 **Convention:** R programmers prefer `<-` over `=`. Variable names are **case-sensitive**: `age` and `Age` are different variables.

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'name   <- "Alice"
age    <- 25
height <- 5.6    # in feet

# Print variables
print(name)
print(age)

# Combine into sentences
paste("Name:", name)
paste(name, "is", age, "years old")',
  id = "ex3_vars"
)
```

Variables make calculations readable and reusable:

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'radius        <- 5
area          <- pi * radius^2
circumference <- 2 * pi * radius

cat("Radius:       ", radius, "\\n")
cat("Area:         ", round(area, 2), "\\n")
cat("Circumference:", round(circumference, 2), "\\n")

# Update the variable — all calculations use the new value
radius <- 10
cat("New area with radius 10:", round(pi * radius^2, 2), "\\n")',
  id = "ex3_calc"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
practice_block(
  placeholder = '# Create variables for a city and its population
city       <- "New York"
population <- 8336817

# Print a sentence like: "New York has a population of 8336817"
print(paste(city, "has a population of", population))',
  id = "pr3",
  prompt = "Create variables for a city name and its population. Then print: <em>\"[City] has a population of [number]\"</em>.",
  hint = "Text goes in quotes: <code>city &lt;- \"Tokyo\"</code><br>
          Numbers don't need quotes: <code>population &lt;- 13960000</code><br>
          Combine with: <code>paste(city, \"has a population of\", population)</code>"
)
```

---

# Chapter 4 — Data Types

R has several core data types:

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `numeric` | Numbers (decimals OK) | `3.14`, `42` |
| `integer` | Whole numbers only | `5L`, `100L` |
| `character` | Text strings | `"hello"` |
| `logical` | TRUE or FALSE | `TRUE`, `FALSE` |
| `NA` | Missing value | `NA` |

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'x <- 3.14
y <- "hello"
z <- TRUE
n <- 5L       # the L makes it an integer

class(x)      # "numeric"
class(y)      # "character"
class(z)      # "logical"
class(n)      # "integer"

is.numeric(x)
is.character(y)
is.logical(z)',
  id = "ex4_types"
)
```

You can convert between types using `as.*()` functions:

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'as.numeric("3.14")    # string → number
as.character(100)     # number → string
as.logical(1)         # 1 → TRUE
as.logical(0)         # 0 → FALSE
as.integer(3.99)      # decimal → integer (truncates, not rounds!)

# NA — the missing value
score <- NA
is.na(score)          # check if value is missing',
  id = "ex4_convert"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
practice_block(
  placeholder = '# Create one variable of each type
my_number  <- 42
my_text    <- "hello R"
my_boolean <- TRUE

# Check their types
class(my_number)
class(my_text)
class(my_boolean)

# Convert my_number to character
as.character(my_number)',
  id = "pr4",
  prompt = "Create a numeric, character, and logical variable. Use <code>class()</code> to verify each type, then convert the number to a character string.",
  hint = "Try <code>my_num &lt;- 42</code>, <code>my_text &lt;- \"hello\"</code>, <code>my_bool &lt;- TRUE</code>.
          Then call <code>class()</code> on each, and <code>as.character(my_num)</code>."
)
```

---

# Chapter 5 — Vectors

A **vector** is R's most fundamental data structure — an ordered list of values, all of the same type. Create vectors with `c()` (short for "combine").

> Almost everything in R is a vector — even a single number like `42` is a length-1 vector. This is what makes R so powerful for data analysis.

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'scores <- c(85, 92, 78, 95, 88)

length(scores)      # number of elements
scores[1]           # first element — R starts counting at 1!
scores[3]           # third element
scores[c(1, 3)]     # first and third
scores[2:4]         # elements 2 through 4
scores[-1]          # everything except the first

# Named vector
grades <- c(Alice = 90, Bob = 85, Carol = 92)
grades["Alice"]',
  id = "ex5_vec"
)
```

Vector operations apply to **every element at once** — this is called **vectorization**:

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'temps_f <- c(72, 68, 75, 80, 71, 77, 73)

# Convert all Fahrenheit temps to Celsius in one line!
(temps_f - 32) * 5/9

# Filter with conditions
temps_f[temps_f > 74]
temps_f[temps_f >= 71 & temps_f <= 77]

# Useful sequences
1:10
seq(0, 1, by = 0.25)
rep("A", 5)',
  id = "ex5_ops"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
practice_block(
  placeholder = '# Create a vector of 5 exam scores
scores <- c(76, 88, 92, 65, 84)

# 1. Print the 2nd score
scores[2]

# 2. Print all scores above 80
scores[scores > 80]

# 3. Reverse the vector
rev(scores)',
  id = "pr5",
  prompt = "Create a vector of 5 exam scores. Then: (1) print the 2nd score, (2) print all scores above 80, (3) print the vector in reverse using <code>rev()</code>.",
  hint = "<code>scores[2]</code> → 2nd element<br>
          <code>scores[scores &gt; 80]</code> → filter condition<br>
          <code>rev(scores)</code> → reverse"
)
```

---

# Chapter 6 — Built-in Functions

R ships with hundreds of built-in functions. Here are the essentials for numeric data:

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `sum(x)` | Sum of all values |
| `mean(x)` | Average |
| `median(x)` | Middle value |
| `min(x)` / `max(x)` | Smallest / largest |
| `sd(x)` | Standard deviation |
| `round(x, n)` | Round to n decimal places |
| `sort(x)` | Sort values |
| `summary(x)` | Quick statistical summary |

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'test_scores <- c(85, 92, 78, 95, 88, 74, 91, 83, 76, 89)

cat("Count:   ", length(test_scores), "\\n")
cat("Sum:     ", sum(test_scores), "\\n")
cat("Mean:    ", mean(test_scores), "\\n")
cat("Median:  ", median(test_scores), "\\n")
cat("Min:     ", min(test_scores), "\\n")
cat("Max:     ", max(test_scores), "\\n")
cat("Std Dev: ", round(sd(test_scores), 2), "\\n")

summary(test_scores)   # all in one!',
  id = "ex6_stats"
)
```

R also has great string functions:

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'msg <- "Hello, R World!"

nchar(msg)                        # character count
toupper(msg)                      # uppercase
tolower(msg)                      # lowercase
substring(msg, 1, 5)             # extract "Hello"
gsub("World", "Student", msg)    # replace text
strsplit("a,b,c,d", ",")        # split by comma
paste("Score:", 95)              # join text and number
paste0("report_", "2024", ".csv") # join without spaces',
  id = "ex6_strings"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
practice_block(
  placeholder = '# Monthly rainfall (mm) for a city
rain_mm <- c(42, 35, 28, 15, 8, 3, 1, 4, 18, 40, 52, 48)

# Total annual rainfall
sum(rain_mm)

# Driest and wettest month values
min(rain_mm)
max(rain_mm)

# Average monthly rainfall (rounded to 1 decimal)
round(mean(rain_mm), 1)',
  id = "pr6",
  prompt = "Using the rainfall vector below, find: (1) total annual rainfall, (2) driest and wettest month values, (3) average monthly rainfall rounded to 1 decimal.",
  hint = "Apply each function directly to <code>rain_mm</code>: <code>sum(rain_mm)</code>, <code>min(rain_mm)</code>, <code>max(rain_mm)</code>, <code>round(mean(rain_mm), 1)</code>"
)
```

---

# Chapter 7 — Data Frames

A **data frame** is R's version of a spreadsheet table. Rows = observations, columns = variables. Each column can hold a different type, but all must have the same length.

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'students <- data.frame(
  name   = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"),
  age    = c(21, 22, 20, 23, 21),
  score  = c(88, 74, 92, 81, 95),
  passed = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE)
)

students           # view the table
nrow(students)     # number of rows
ncol(students)     # number of columns
names(students)    # column names
str(students)      # structure overview',
  id = "ex7_df"
)
```

Access rows, columns, and filter data:

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'students <- data.frame(
  name  = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"),
  age   = c(21, 22, 20, 23, 21),
  score = c(88, 74, 92, 81, 95)
)

students$name            # a column using $
students$score
students[1, ]            # first row (all columns)
students[, "score"]      # score column by name
students[3, "name"]      # Carolâ€™s name specifically

# Filter: only students who scored >= 88
students[students$score >= 88, ]
mean(students$score)',
  id = "ex7_access"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
practice_block(
  placeholder = 'books <- data.frame(
  title  = c("The Hobbit", "Dune", "1984", "Brave New World"),
  author = c("Tolkien", "Herbert", "Orwell", "Huxley"),
  year   = c(1937, 1965, 1949, 1932),
  pages  = c(310, 688, 328, 311)
)

# View the data frame
books

# Find the longest book (pages)
max(books$pages)

# Average pages across all books
round(mean(books$pages), 1)

# Filter: books published before 1950
books[books$year < 1950, ]',
  id = "pr7",
  prompt = "A book data frame is pre-filled. View it, find the longest book (max pages), calculate average pages, and filter books published before 1950.",
  hint = "Access a column: <code>books$pages</code><br>
          Max pages: <code>max(books$pages)</code><br>
          Filter by year: <code>books[books$year &lt; 1950, ]</code>"
)
```

---

# Chapter 8 — Visualization

R has powerful built-in plotting. Create professional charts in 1–2 lines of code.

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'x <- 1:10
y <- x^2

plot(x, y,
     main = "y = x squared",
     xlab = "x",
     ylab = "x^2",
     col  = "steelblue",
     pch  = 16,
     type = "b",    # both points and lines
     lwd  = 2)',
  id = "ex8_scatter"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
example_block(
'par(mfrow = c(1, 2))   # 1 row, 2 columns of plots

# Bar chart
sales <- c(Jan=120, Feb=95, Mar=140, Apr=160, May=130)
barplot(sales, col = "coral", main = "Monthly Sales",
        ylab = "Units", las = 2)

# Histogram of simulated data
set.seed(42)
heights <- rnorm(200, mean = 170, sd = 10)
hist(heights, col = "skyblue", main = "Height Distribution",
     xlab = "Height (cm)", breaks = 15)',
  id = "ex8_bar"
)
```

```{r results='asis'}
practice_block(
  placeholder = '# Monthly average temperatures (Jan-Dec)
months <- 1:12
temps  <- c(35, 38, 48, 58, 67, 76, 81, 79, 71, 60, 49, 38)

plot(months, temps,
     type = "l",
     lwd  = 2,
     col  = "tomato",
     main = "Monthly Temperatures",
     xlab = "Month",
     ylab = "Temp (F)",
     xaxt = "n")

axis(1, at = 1:12, labels = month.abb)
points(months, temps, pch = 16, col = "tomato")',
  id = "pr8",
  prompt = "Modify the temperature data for your own city and change the line color. Try color names like <code>\"steelblue\"</code>, <code>\"forestgreen\"</code>, or <code>\"darkorchid\"</code>.",
  hint = "Change the values in <code>temps</code> to your city's temperatures.
          Change <code>col = \"tomato\"</code> to any R color name.
          Update <code>main</code> to your city's name."
)
```

---

> 🎉 **Congratulations!** You've covered the core building blocks of R: output, arithmetic, variables, types, vectors, functions, data frames, and plotting. 
>
> **Next steps:** Explore [tidyverse](https://www.tidyverse.org/), [ggplot2](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/), and real datasets on [Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets).
